It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South: Environmental civil litigation by prosecutors in China compared to Brazil Yifan Shi School of Political Science and Public Administration, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, China Benjamin van Rooij School of Law, University of California at Irvine, Irvine, CA, USA Abstract Brazil’s successful prosecutorial civil action against polluters could be a regulatory example for the Global South. This paper analyses whether such regulation could also develop without the major political, institutional, and legal reforms that spurred it in Brazil. To do so, it analyzes China, where similar reforms have so far not occurred, but where prosecutors have recently started to initiate civil litigation against polluters. It ﬁnds that prosecutorial civil litigation in China has only a limited regulatory effect or potential. Prosecutors in China are inﬂuenced by conﬂicting incentive structures that reward one-off lower level test cases with an innovation bonus, while structurally stimulating a focus on general crime ﬁghting. Ironically, as a result of such incentives, the recent legal reform, toward providing standing for prosecutors in public interest litigation, will, in contrast to Brazil, decrease rather than increase the regulatory effect of these cases. These ﬁndings have implications for understanding how the interaction between regulatory independence, legal reform, and regime type shapes possibilities for regulatory innovation in the Global South. Keywords: China, environmental law, Global South, pollution, prosecutors. 1. Introduction In the Global South, environmental governmental regulators are often obstructed in their enforcement work by a lack of independence and capacity, while having to face widespread and structural non-compliance (McAllister et al. 2010). An important way to deal with these implementation challenges has been to diversify regulatory actors and mechanisms to circumvent and bolster faltering governmental regulators. This has led to projects with market based or society based forms of regulation. Unfortunately, few of these projects have been able to yield a nationwide, veriﬁable, and long-term sustainable impact to end pervasive non-compliance and its resultant pollution. One can wonder whether countries where illegal pollution is common business practice and where citizens often lack informational, organizational, legal, and political resources to take effective action, are ready for market and social instruments. In the Global South, perhaps regulatory innovation should be sought not in society or the market, but with justice institutions, such as courts and prosecutors. In their study of Indian regulation of the telecommunications sector, Thiruvengadam and Joshi, for example, point to the importance of the Supreme Court in bolstering weak state regulators (2012). They point to how the Court’s institutional independence, as well as its particular authority, played a vital role in its function to strengthen and legitimize telecom regulation. They conclude that while scholars “focus on the rise of the regulatory state in the Global South, they should focus in particular on the role of judiciaries (especially in jurisdictions where judicial systems have established some institutional cred- ibility and independence) and on other institutions that could play a supportive or facilitative role similar to that which we have described” (Thiruvengadam & Joshi 2012, p. 341). Email: bvanrooij@ law.uci.edu Accepted for publication 4 May 2014. bs_bs_banner Regulation & Governance (2016) doi:10.1111/rego.12112 10, 44–57 © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Page 2: In the realm of environmental regulation in the Global South, the prime case of justice actors strengthening weak regulation is Brazil. Here prosecutors started to enforce environmental law through civil action. It is a clear example where a successful regulatory change ended a vicious circle of weak enforcement and widespread compliance, and overcame structural close ties between government regulators and regulated enterprises (McAllister 2008). The success of prosecutors in Brazil as environmental regulators is nothing but astounding. It shows that, in the environmental ﬁeld, a prosecutorial form of regulation may be highly relevant. All industrialized or rapidly indus- trializing countries in the Global South could look to it for guidance. A key question is whether something similar could happen in other Southern contexts or whether this is just speciﬁc to Brazil. This paper seeks to explore the relevance of the Brazilian experience for other Southern contexts. In particular, this paper looks at whether prosecutors could start to regulate environmental pollution through civil litigation, when the major political reform and the resultant creation of prosecutorial independence and special prosecutorial civil litigation powers that were vital in the Brazilian case do not take place. To do so, it studies China, where prosecutors have, in a limited number of cases, started to initiate civil litigation against polluters, acting themselves as plaintiffs (Liu 2011). They have done so in a context where there has not been a major political change toward democrati- zation, nor has there been a major institutional reform creating institutional or personal independence for pros- ecutors.1 Until very recently, Chinese prosecutors did not explicitly have the legal authority to have standing to initiate these cases as plaintiffs (Liu 2011). The paper analyses whether these Chinese cases mark the start of a broader regulatory shift, as happened in Brazil, and, thus, whether the Brazilian example can also function in a context without regime change and its resultant prosecutorial independence and expanded civil litigation authority. By analyzing China and comparing it to Brazil, the paper seeks to reﬂect upon the role that independence and legal standing play in the development and functioning of prosecutorial forms of civil environmental regulation. Fur- thermore, it seeks to draw theoretical lessons about how regime type and regulatory development and legal and regulatory change are related. Finally, the paper draws out broader implications about the role of justice institutions as regulators in the Global South. The paper uses different sources of data. First, it uses data from newspapers, websites, and other publicly available materials to analyze the basic characteristics and (where possible) outcomes of the existing 24 publicized instances of prosecutorial public interest litigation against pollution in China.Although it is possible that there are other cases, this is the most comprehensive collection of such cases to date. Second, as background material to understand the basic traits of these cases it uses information gathered through interviews with 18 prosecutors, two judges, and two academics, all of whom were not directly involved in these cases, but understand the context in which they occur.2 2. Comparing preconditions for prosecutorial regulation Brazil’s successful prosecutorial regulation of environmental pollution has had several preconditions, which have been best summarized by McAllister (2008) and Mueller (2006). First, there was a need for a new regulator to act where the existing administrative authorities had failed to do so and where, as a result, environmental degradation and pollution had become highly severe (McAllister 2008, p. 20). Second, prosecutors through their historic design already had latent powers to oversee the general interest and intervene in private litigation (McAllister 2008, p. 61; Mueller 2006, p. 15). Third, since the 1980s, the Ministério Público (MP) gained an extremely strong form of institutional and personal independence (McAllister 2008, pp. 63–65; Mueller 2006, p. 16). They did so as the 1988 Brazilian constitution made the MP highly independent from other branches of government, and, thus, also more insulated from undue inﬂuence from industrial enterprises with close governmental ties (McAllister 2008, p. 110). Important elements of the MP’s independence include its own separated power distinct from the other three branches, budget guarantees and autonomy, and strong personal tenure for prosecutors (McAllister 2008, pp. 63–65). This has resulted not only in institutional independence of the MP from other branches of power, but also personal independence with prosecutors acting free of undue inﬂuence from their leadership (McAllister 2008, pp. 108–109). Such independence was vital in the successful function of the MP: the more independent the prosecutors, the more effective their regulatory work (McAllister 2008, p. 87; Mueller 2006, p. 16) Fourth, in 1981, the MP gained the power to initiate, not just to intervene in, civil litigation to protect environmental interests, a power broadened in 1985 to cover widespread public civil action to sue those that damage the public interest (McAllister 2008, pp. 62–63), and institutionalized in the 1988 Constitution (Mueller 2006, p. 15).3 Fifth, the political reform of the 1980s Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South Y. Shi and B. van Rooij © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 45 Page 3: ending military dictatorship and starting democratization created the environment in which the MP lobbied for and got its enhanced independence and civil action powers (McAllister 2008, pp. 74–75). The context of the civil litigation cases in China is both similar to and different from Brazil. We shall ﬁrst look at the similarities (regulatory failure and historic design) and then at the differences (no political reform, no reform toward independence, and no legal reform [until very recently] creating standing for prosecutors in civil litigation). 2.1. Similarities: Regulatory failure and prosecutorial civil oversight functions in China Environmental law enforcement by China’s environmental protection bureaus (EPBs) has been notoriously chal- lenging (Ma & Ortolano 2000; Tilt 2007). EPBs have insufﬁcient staff and equipment to properly inspect an ever-increasing number of potential polluters. Moreover, sanctions generally remain weak. The average environ- mental ﬁnes for 2006, the last year for which data were still released, were only 10,000 RMB or about 1,100 euro (SEPA 2007). Sanctions are weak, not just because the maximum legally allowed ﬁnes remain quite low, but more importantly, because locally managed and paid enforcement agents are inﬂuenced by their local governments and even by perverse central level incentives (Ran 2013) not to enforce national laws that run against local interests. While there is obviously variation geographically and over time, we can generally conclude that administrative enforcement of environmental law in China is challenged by a lack of capacity and regulatory autonomy. China, just as Brazil, lacks a governmental regulator that has sufﬁcient capacity and independence to successfully enforce and, thus, implement environmental law. Citizens and sometimes non-governmental organizations (NGOs) (Stern 2013, p. 118) have started to take action, taking some polluters to court. However, their actions have not yet yielded a strong regulatory effect, as cases are still rare and chances of citizens winning and actually ending and preventing pollution are slim (Wang 2006–2007; Stern 2013). China, similar to Brazil, has a need for a new type of regulator to step in to help implement environmental law. This need is well recognized in China, and over the last decade Chinese scholars and some environmental regulators have argued for procuratorates to start using civil litigation to help regulate environmental risks and, thus, help deal with China’s environmental regulatory deﬁcit (Jiang & Duan 2000; Chen 2005; Sun 2009; Tang 2010; Stern 2013). Similar to Brazilian prosecutors, Chinese prosecutorial agencies also function to oversee the legal quality of civil litigation.4 They have several powers to do so. First, Chinese prosecutors have a strong authority overseeing the quality of court judgments in civil cases through so-called Kangsu (literally“protest litigation”) procedures, in which they review the court’s decision and can order a retrial (Liu 2011).5 Second, prosecutors may also support or urge litigation by citizens, state organs, or public organizations when their civil rights or interests of the state have been harmed (Liu 2011).6,7 The regulatory potential of these powers to oversee and support civil litigation is enhanced because Chinese tort law allows not merely for compensation and repair of damages, but also for remedies that can end and prevent dangers at hand and, thus, potentially regulate environmental risks.8 In order to exercise such civil law authorities, Chinese procurators have developed specialized civil law divisions and developed civil litigation expertise and experience. 2.2. Differences: Institutional, legal, and political reforms China is, of course, different from Brazil in a number of crucial aspects. First, Chinese prosecutors do not have a degree of institutional or individual independence comparable to Brazil. Although the Chinese constitution pre- scribes that procuratorates shall exercise their power independently, the reality is markedly different.9 The same constitution itself already speciﬁes that the People’s Procuratorates are“state organs,”that are supervised both by the people’s congresses at their level of jurisdiction, as well as superior procuratorates.10,11 In everyday practice, Chinese procuratorates are most strongly inﬂuenced by the government and Politics and Law Commission at their own level of jurisdiction and to a lesser extent by superior procuratorates (Jin 1981; Song 2004), in a way that is highly similar to the oversight of courts (Peerenboom 2002; Liebman 2007) and other governmental agencies (Saich 2011) in China. Prosecutors do not have strong individual operational independence (Chen & Li 2006), nor enjoy tenure or other guarantees to enhance individual autonomy as exist in Brazil, but instead are intensively managed through cadre evaluation mechanisms (ZJW02112011)12 that exist throughout the Chinese bureaucracy (Edin 2003; Heimer 2006; Landry 2008).13 Moreover, Chinese local governments that inﬂuence Chinese procuratorates and prosecutors have close ties to industrial enterprises responsible for pollution and are well known to protect local interests, rather than enforce national environmental law. Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South Y. Shi and B. van Rooij © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 46 Page 4: A second core difference with Brazil is that until very recently there has been no standing for prosecutors in civil litigation in China. The 1991 Civil Procedural Law in article 108 stipulates that only citizens, legal persons, or other organizations with a “direct interest in the case” can act as plaintiffs. As such, China’s civil procedure law does not recognize the broad standing for prosecutors to initiate civil litigation against polluters for defending the public interest, as Brazil has introduced with the 1981 National Environmental Policy Act and the 1985 Public Civil Action Act (McAllister 2008, pp. 62–63). After years of debate amongst scholars, prosecutors, judges, and environmental agents, Chinese Civil Procedure Law was recently amended to indicate a move toward a broader standing that might come to include prosecutors. Article 55 of the 2012 amended version of the Civil Procedure Law stipulates that “relevant organs and organizations prescribed by law may initiate lawsuit at competent courts against conducts jeopardizing the public interests, such as causing environment pollution or damaging the interests of a large number of consumers.” All is not yet fully clear though, as while some hold that the new provision provides standing to procuratorates (Li 2012), others, including members of the National People’s Congress Standing Committee and judicial practitioners, ﬁnd that the rule is still too ambiguous to determine who exactly has such right of standing (Li 2012). According to China’s prosecutor-general, the new law does not yet provide standing to the procuratorates to act as plaintiffs in civil litigation cases, and prosecutors should restrict their involvement in such cases (Cao 2012). An example of how speciﬁc legislation may narrow standing is the June 2013 second new draft for an amended Environmental Protection Law reserving it solely to the All China Environmental Federation, a government-run NGO (Chen 2013) and not to the procuratorates (or civic society organizations). The limited prosecutorial independence and the limited standing in civil litigation are closely linked to the third key difference between Brazil and China: the fact that China has not undergone major political change in any way comparable to the democratization processes following military rule in Brazil. China remains a one-party state and has maintained a resilient form of authoritarianism (Nathan 2003). A prime aspect of the party state is to maintain control over branches of government, including the People’s Procuratorates. Therefore, it is unlikely that without a regime-type change, a true change in the independence of prosecutors, both institutionally or individually, can take place. Moreover, a one-party state will likely be highly restrictive in developing broad standing in civil litigation, not merely for legal powers granted, but also in how they are exercised, so as to prevent a broader questioning or even undermining of the party-state powers and interests by those authorized to protect the general interest. 3. Descriptive analysis of prosecutorial environmental civil litigation in China China has, thus, not had a sweeping political change creating independent prosecutors tasked and authorized to use civil litigation against environmental pollution as occurred in Brazil. Nevertheless, in the last few years there has been a nascent development of prosecutorial civil litigation that bears some resemblance to Brazil. In about 24 instances, prosecutors have started to use civil litigation to sue polluters as plaintiffs, doing so to defend the public interest (Liu 2011; Stern 2013). This might indicate the start of prosecutorial civil litigation similar to Brazil in China. It might also show that political, institutional, and legal reforms are not necessary conditions for the Brazilian experience to be replicated elsewhere. To evaluate these ideas we must analyze to what extent the current Chinese cases are or may lead to a regulatory development that is comparable to prosecutorial environmental regulation in Brazil. Before directly answering this question and comparing the current Chinese cases, as well as their future potential with Brazil, this section ﬁrst provides a descriptive analysis of the cases that have occurred so far, analyzing in which types of cases Chinese prosecutors have initiated litigation against polluters. It analyses a unique dataset that consists of all publically available cases where prosecutors were involved in environmental civil litigation. The dataset contains cases up to March 2013, although there may be further cases that we were unable to discover.14 As we had to rely on published records for these cases instead of on-the-ground interviews with those involved, there is still much we are unable to analyze, including the exact perceptions and motivations of those involved, as well as a thorough understanding of what gave rise to the cases, what happened during trial or mediation, and what the longer term effects have been. To enable descriptive analysis, we have coded the cases for key variables. Variables were selected on the basis of their relevance for understanding the regulatory need and potential of prosecutorial civil litigation against pollution in these cases. The need and potential for prosecutorial civil litigation is greatest in cases where EPBs and citizens have been unable or unwilling to act, and where prosecutors can and will successfully act. Key variables to analyze © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 47 Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South Y. Shi and B. van Rooij Page 5: this are: the overall number of such cases (the higher the more potential deterrent effect of cases); the geographical dispersion of the cases (the more dispersed and also the more in rural and poorer areas, the more need and potential); the type of damages (the more severe and harder to detect, the more need and potential); the existence of victims (the more, the less need); the types of polluters (the more state-owned and larger ﬁrms, the more need); the involvement of other actors, such as EPBs, local governments, higher level procurators, or the media (the more involvement, the less need); the types of claims (the more reparative/preventive, the more the regulatory potential of these cases); and case outcomes (the more success and the higher the damages awarded, the more deterrent potential). Our ﬁrst ﬁnding is that the overall number of prosecutorial civil litigation cases that we have been able to ﬁnd against pollution is very low, 24 by the end of 2012. If we look at the distribution over time (Fig. 1), we see that after two cases in 2003, environmental civil litigation took off in earnest in 2008, with a steady increase from 2010 to 2012, from three to seven cases per year. The data do not reveal a clear predilection in terms of the geographical location of the cases in our sample. If we analyze their general distribution (see Fig. 2), we note that they are centered mostly in the east, south-east, and south-western areas of China. There have been no cases in our sample in the north-west, north, and north-east. We can see that the cases cover the afﬂuent (both in absolute and per capita gross domestic product [GDP]15) (Gua- ngdong, Jiangsu, Shandong, Zhejiang, Fujian), middle-income (Sichuan, Hunan, Hubei, Chongqing, Jiangxi), and poorest provinces (Yunnan and Guizhou). All cases we found involve one-off efforts with no follow-up. With the exception of Guangdong and Zhejiang provinces, most provinces only had one or two cases. Looking in more detail, we ﬁnd that none of the procuratorates worked on more than one case, except for the Fanyu District Procuratorate in Guangzhou City that worked on two (cases 7 and 23). This is noteworthy, especially in Jiaxing City, where there was a total of four prosecutorial environmental civil litigation cases, but each time in different districts and counties. Analyzing the case details about where the actual damages occurred and where the victims lived, we ﬁnd that 75 percent (n = 18) occurred in rural areas, while 25 percent (n = 6) occurred in clearly urban areas.16 Most cases were, thus, highly local and rural. Analyzing which procuratorates were involved in our sample, we see that the cases overwhelmingly involve local district-level procuratorates, with only two cases involving municipal intermediate-level procuratorates, while there were no cases with higher provincial-level procuratorates.17 In some of the cases, higher-level support for the local-level cases played a role. In those cases, we see that the local-level procuratorates were coordinated to some extent by higher-level procuratorates or at least sought approval at the higher level. In case 6 in Jiangxi, for instance, the municipal-level procuratorate prepared the case together with the local-level EPB and, once it had sufﬁcient evidence, assigned it to the district-level procuratorate. Another example is case 17 in Zhejiang province, where the formal plaintiff was the district procuratorate, the case was led by the municipal-level procuratorate, and the provincial level had authorized an opinion supporting the district level’s role as plaintiff. Also we see that, for instance, in Guangzhou City, after the second prosecutorial environ- mental civil litigation case occurred within the municipal jurisdiction (case 7), the municipal procuratorate drafted a guidance ordinance for further environmental civil litigation there. Such higher level coordination exists in the Chinese constitutional set-up of procuratorial work integration,” making lower level procuratorates subordinate to instructions from superior level procuratorates.18 This has led to a practice where higher and lower level prosecutors, especially from the weaker administrative and civil divisions, meet to discuss complicated cases where lower level prosecutors have to accept guidance and advice from their higher level colleagues.19 In many cases, prosecutors collaborated informally with environmental authorities. At ﬁrst impression, the data show that, in most cases, prosecutors acted as sole plaintiffs (Fig. 2). In two cases they acted to formally support civil litigation of an EPB and Land and Resource Bureau (both in Yunnan province), in one case in Guangdong province the procuratorate urged a town government to initiate civil litigation, and in one case in Daoxian (in Hunan province) it asked the court to enforce an administrative decision by a local EPB.20 There is evidence, however, that some of these cases could only develop because the prosecutors were able to work with the environmental authorities. A good example is the Xinyu municipal people’s procuratorate in Jiangxi (case 6) collaborating to gather evidence for a civil litigation case that was ultimately ﬁled by the Yushui district people’s procuratorate. The data reveal that the cases were tried in three different types of courts: ordinary courts, maritime courts, and environmental tribunals. All except three were dealt with in the ordinary courts, two in the maritime courts, and one in an environmental tribunal. The low number of cases in the environmental courts is noteworthy, as these recently established, specialized, green-oriented branches of the courts would seem to be ideally equipped and open to such new forms of litigation, especially because some of these courts adapted special procedures that allow standing for prosecutors as plaintiffs in environmental civil litigation, regardless of the pre-2012 Civil Procedure Law’s insistence that plaintiffs must have a direct interest at stake (Liu 2012; Stern 2013). Most of the cases involved relatively small and weak defendants. We distinguish three main types for analytical purposes. The ﬁrst type is the Individual Defendant, a single person or small group of individuals who have somehow caused pollution, but do not act as an enterprise. Second, are Micro Enterprises, as deﬁned under Chinese law, with the lowest possible requirements for entities still called enterprises, primary income, and assets.21 Third is a rest category that includes all forms of enterprises larger than micro-enterprises as deﬁned under Chinese law, here called Major Enterprises. This category includes what Chinese law would call large, medium, and small enterprises.22 In our sample, most defendants are either individuals or micro-businesses (see Fig. 3). Only four enterprises fall within our broad category of major enterprises, including a cement plant in Hunan (case 3), a chemical plant in Chongqing (case 10), two poultry farm companies in Yunnan (case 13), and an oil and gas company in Guangdong (case 16). In case 12, in Guangdong province, there was a mixture of defendants with both individuals and one major enterprise. It is noteworthy that none of the defendants were state owned or even collectively owned, which are more likely to have close governmental ties and are, therefore, more difﬁcult for the EPBs to act against. All of the cases were against privately owned ﬁrms or individuals. In most cases, there were victims of pollution: in 17 of the 24 cases. In all of these cases the victims were located in the direct vicinity of the source of the pollution, while in one case in Sichuan province (case 2), the procuratorate © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 49 Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South Y. In this last case, before the procuratorate moved to this neighborhood, the neighbors’ health had been affected by the pollution, and citizens complained in vain for many years to the local EPB while the pollution continued. Once the procuratorate started developing health problems, prosecutors initiated the public interest suit as plaintiff. Therefore, civil litigation by procuratorates very often involved cases where there were victims who themselves could also have sought damages directly through ordinary litigation. The cases chieﬂy involved water pollution and, less so, air, soil and noise pollution. Figure 4 outlines the different types of damage involved in all cases, calculating the numbers based on the occurrence of each type of damage, including multiple damage in several cases. There were also some cases that involved damage to natural resources, which have been listed under the “Other” category. In all, we see that damage, which normally generates more public complaints, as it is generally easier to detect (Van Rooij 2006), such as air and noise pollution, yields fewer civil litigation cases with procuratorates. The cases mostly concerned relatively minor damage. Minor damage is deﬁned here as pollution resulting in economic losses below 10,000 RMB and with no known negative health effects. Major damage involves more than 10,000 RMB or known negative health effects.23 This is a conservative standard, in that it easily deﬁnes cases as major. Nevertheless, we still ﬁnd that two-thirds of the cases involve minor damage while one-third involves major damage.24 Media attention for the pollution prior to ﬁling the case does not seem to play a role in spurring the public interest suits initiated by procuratorates. Pollution had been reported in a local newspaper prior to the litigation in only one case in Guangdong province. We found no similar reports for any of the other cases. We also found no intensive national-level media attention for the pollution prior to prosecutorial involvement in any of the cases. Ex post media attention is a different story. Most often the original story was posted by the procuratorates themselves online, and then later picked up by local newspapers and, in some cases, larger national news web portals and environmental websites. In the cases studied, we see that there is often a mixture of different claims. In many cases we ﬁnd a combination of claims for reparative and preventive remedies to clean up, end, and prevent pollution, as well as claims for Figure 3 Types of defendants. On the whole, 54 percent (n = 13) make sole reparative/preventive claims, 29 percent (n = 7) make a combination of reparative/preventive and compensatory claims, while only 17 percent (n = 4) make other claims. Compared to data we have about ordinary environmental litigation, the rate of reparative/preventive claims seems somewhat higher; when compared to a sample of such ordinary cases published online in Chinalawinfo.com.cn, 41 percent had sole compensatory claims, 32 percent had a mix of reparative/preventive and compensatory claims, and there were no sole injunctive cases (Altena 2010). To understand the results, let us now look at what types of procedures were followed to resolve the disputes. In Figure 5, we see that the majority of cases was either adjudicated (10 cases) or mediated (11 cases). In one case, we were unable to trace the outcome (case 22 in Zhejiang), in one the procuratorate withdrew the case (case 20 in Zhejiang against an individual called Zhang with no direct victims), and in one case (case 18, against an oil reﬁnery in Hunan province) the procuratorate asked the court to enforce an EPB decision to end pollution. Broadly speaking, prosecutors have been very successful in winning adjudication or achieving favorable settle- ments in our sample of cases. Our data allow us a limited view into the substantive outcome of the cases. For many cases (all except case 21), we have details about what the ﬁnal verdict or mediation agreement was. In all of those cases, we can generally conclude that the procuratorates formally won (in the adjudication cases) or received a favorable settlement (in mediation). We also see many cases in which major compensation awards were granted (cases 3, 5, 7, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 17), for instance, in case 15 where six individual defendants in Yunnan province had to pay 440,000 yuan to a special civil litigation fund, or in a mediation case, number 15, where the defendants agreed to pay 540,000 yuan for environmental improvement. To compare our ﬁndings with an analysis of ordinary cases, we have three sources of data. First is Lü (2008), the most comprehensive set, however, she only analyzed the cases with an overall win or lose outcome, ﬁnding that about 50 percent of citizens generally win. Second, we have Altena’s (2010) analysis of a smaller number of cases published online in a legal case law database, analyzing not just the general outcome, but also the percentage of compensatory claims awarded. She ﬁnds that only 16 percent of cases get 100 percent of the damages they claim, seven percent get 67–99 percent, and 22 percent get only one to 33 percent, while 22 percent simply lose and get nothing. Stern (2013, pp. 139– 140) similarly ﬁnds that in a broad sample of citizen environmental law suits studied, plaintiffs were awarded 29 percent of their claims, by judges using pragmatism, common sense, and wide discretion based on what they think polluters can afford, placing part of the blame on the plaintiffs, and limiting compensation when they fear plaintiffs are over-claiming. Althena and Stern, thus, show that in many cases, winning does not necessarily mean that plaintiffs get most of what they claimed. For 25 percent (n = 6) of the limited number of cases studied here, we know both the compensation claim and the actual damages awarded. In all of these cases (nos. 5, 7, 15, 17, 24) 100 percent of the originally claimed damages was awarded, and in one case even more than was claimed (case 13). To summarize, we can descriptively characterize prosecutorial civil litigation against pollution: few cases; mostly rural location; conducted by local-level prosecutors with no prior experience in such cases; sometimes guided and supported by higher-level prosecutors; often collaborating informally with EPBs involving minor companies; with minor, mostly water or mixed pollution; with victims; with no prior media attention; involving both compensatory/ reparative and preventive claims/handled through both mediation and adjudication resulting in favorable judg- ments and settlements.
