Discussion: Can China be the next Brazil? Now that we have descriptively looked at the existing Chinese cases we can discuss two questions: First, to what extent are the current cases comparable to prosecutorial civil litigation environmental regulation in Brazil? And second, to what extent might they potentially point to a future development in the direction of Brazil? The core characteristic of Brazil’s prosecutorial form of environmental regulation has been its success in ending impunity of illegal polluters. In the cases we were able to study in China, the success prosecutors have in winning civil litigation cases and getting more than just compensation for damages, but also remedies to repair and prevent harm, is positive. There is potential here, perhaps, as prosecutorial involvement in environmental civil litigation cases might have an extra-deterrent effect (GZG25102011,ZJW02112011). As an expert on these cases explained to us: “Enterprises do not fear environmental civil litigation cases, what they fear is the involvement of procuratorates. Why do they fear that? Well if they continue violations, what would the prosecutors do next? They would criminally prosecute enterprise leadership” (ZJW02112011). However, any comparison with current civil litigation in China and the broad regulatory success of such cases in Brazil ends given the low number, as well as the type of existing cases in China, at least in as far as we were able to ﬁnd them. There are several hundreds of thousands of environmental complaints each year; however, prosecutors initiate very few of these.Also, in the sample studied here there have been no landmark or breakthrough cases yet, where one local case was picked up strongly in the national media and might create a broader ripple effect (cf.Stern 2013,p.146) deterring potential polluters (cf. Liu 2011). Even within the small number of cases studied, very few involved major or difﬁcult cases of pollution that could not have been solved otherwise. Most cases had relatively minor polluters with limited power, victims that could themselves have sued, and had the collaboration of EPBs that might have more easily used their administrative powers to end and prevent the pollution involved (cf. Liu 2011; Stern 2013). Thus, we cannot conclude that these existing cases have much added regulatory value.We see a particular pattern in these cases studied where mainly local-level procuratorates take on cases with mainly minor damages, with minor media attention, and with victims and EPB support. So these are not the large numbers or landmark cases that one would hope or expect to arise in environmental civil litigation.Rather it seems that procuratorates have experimented locally with the lower hanging fruit, the easy cases. Prosecutors have acted in part to add capacity and autonomy where needed, but often where they were not needed. They were needed in the water pollution cases, but less so in cases where there were already victims and EPB support and where there were smaller enterprises, which was in the majority of cases. Because most cases involved grass-roots-level procuratorates, it is unlikely that they added much capacity, and, vice versa, it seems these prosecutors lacked capacity, as evidenced by the fact that they needed the help of the EPBs in almost all cases. Of course, this does show that EPBs have at least been ingenious enough to augment their lack of capacity at times by collaborating with prosecutors, albeit only in relatively minor cases. So do these ﬁrst 24 cases studied here point to a broader development that is likely to take off in the near future and that may yet come to resemble prosecutorial civil litigation in Brazil? To answer this question, we must ask why there were so few cases and why the prosecutors that did try only made one attempt. A ﬁrst reason for the low number of cases generally, as well as the low number per local procuratorate, is that these cases do not ﬁt the local capacity and preferences of prosecutors (cf. Liu 2011). One obstacle in initiating more of these cases is simply that procuratorates are not equipped to handle the specialized environmental cases that often require technical, legal, and scientiﬁc knowledge to gather and present the evidence (Lü 2008; Sun 2009). Of the 16 prosecutors we interviewed who worked in the civil litigation branch from Sichuan province, none indicated experience with initiating envi- ronmental litigation. Only two prosecutors indicated any knowledge of these cases, and both times they mentioned a local Sichuan case (case 2) (LPF30082010,WHB19082010). More importantly, almost none thought prosecutors should take on these cases as plaintiff, pointing at the limits in their own staff, workload, and ability to take on these cases. As a senior trainer of prosecutors explained to us: We always joke that the civil and administrative prosecutors are the old, weak and crippled in our nation’s procuratorates. Many are just before retirement or have very limited ability. For example, in one unit there are seven prosecutors, but only three to four are ﬁt for active duty. If you give these people this new task (environmental civil litigation), they cannot do it. And if you write in the law that procuratorates have the power to initiate civil litigation, I guess the result will be that each jurisdiction does one or two cases and that is the end of it, and they are deemed to have done their legal obligation. (ZJW02112011) © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 52 Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South Y. Shi and B. van Rooij Page 10: This is highly different from the young and energetic prosecutors in Brazil’s MP (McAllister 2008), nor do we see an indication that reformist prosecutors employing a relational form of regulation are at work in these civil law divisions, as Coslovsky found to be a vital element in the Brazilian experience (Coslovsky 2011). Apart from limited capacity, interference by local governments also undermines prosecutorial civil litigation. Although some hold that prosecutors are more insulated from local power arrangements than EPBs, as higher-level procuratorates can maintain stronger oversight over lower-level prosecutors than is possible in the environmental regulatory domain (TWJ031202011), there is also evidence to the contrary. In fact, prosecutors are at times susceptible to interference from local governments trying to protect local industry and block unfavorable litigation against local enterprises, just like the EPBs. As one of our informants, with in-depth knowledge of these cases, told us: If the money comes from him (the local government), and manages your staff, do you dare to sue enterprises that pay him taxes? That is impossible (. . .). So how were the civil litigation cases that did occur possible? Well, here the local government had already given permission to initiate the litigation. If you punish these companies, you can only go ahead if you have the support of the whole local party and government organizations, as well as party committee. Then you will be successful for sure. Without their support you will fail for sure. (ZJW02112011) The lack of capacity and independence from local governments and, thus, local industry do not tell the full story, however. They do not explain why prosecutors who did initiate these cases and ended up winning them did not try again. To understand this we must look at the Chinese cadre evaluation management system (Edin 2003; Heimer 2006; Landry 2008; Minzner 2009; Ran 2013; Wang 2013) that guides the work of local-level prosecutors. A conﬂict between incentives rewarding innovation on the one hand, and incentives rewarding crime control and punishing risk-taking on the other, can explain why local-level prosecutors only took one-off attempts at environmental civil litigation. Under the cadre evaluation rules for prosecutors, innovation is rewarded. These evaluation rules seem to have played a stimulating role in the cases where prosecutors initiated civil litigation. We learned from our infor- mants that the prosecutors that did initiate these cases were stimulated because they could beneﬁt from bonuses for conducting innovative forms of prosecutorial work (ZJW02112011, LZM31102011, TWJ03122011). These bonuses for innovation might also explain why, except in one case, none of the procuratorates did more than one such environmental civil litigation case. As a leading environmental law expert with in-depth knowledge of these cases and contacts with prosecutors involved explains: If you look at cases where the prosecutors were plaintiffs, you see that overall each procuratorate did so only once. Once done, they never did so again. Why? Because doing it again will not yield any innovation bonus. Second time around it is no longer innovative; you have already innovated. (LZM31102011) One can, however, wonder why this incentive system did not create many more one-shot cases in China’s multitude of county-level procurators. The answer is that the incentive to innovate is but one of several indicators used to evaluate cadre. The same cadre evaluation system also incentivizes a focus on crime control and limited risk-taking through crime reduction and case success rate targets. This directly contradicts innovation through environmental civil litigation, which will logically point away from general crime and involves risk. As such, it directly undermines the development of environmental civil litigation suits. As one of our informants explained in some detail: China’s prosecutors and judges are subject to a very strict and unreasonable evaluation system, like an enterprise management target evaluation system. If I, for instance, have a hundred cases this year, you get a set of highly ambitious targets for these cases (. . .) for the criminal section of the procuratorate (these targets) demand that in their public prosecution they should not have any cases where the defendant is judged innocent. If one of my cases ends up with an innocence verdict, I am judged to have failed my case. It is very scary (. . .) So you can imagine that prosecutors do not want to handle more cases, given this system. And if I as a prosecutor start to do civil litigation, you tell me, what would I do if I lose? (. . .) From a civil law perspective losing cases is normal, but from the evaluation perspective, how would a loss be handled? (ZJW02112011) © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 53 Prosecutorial regulation in the Global South Y. Shi and B. van Rooij Page 11: It seems these evaluation systems have given rise to a risk aversion amongst public prosecutors. As a leading environmental law expert who has discussed these cases with local prosecutors concludes: When I asked them (the prosecutors involved) why they only acted against smaller enterprises, they said, we did not dare to take on big ﬁrms, what would we do if we would lose? (LZM31102011) Moreover, an expansion of these civil litigation cases, some claim, may actually undermine prosecutorial power as the prosecutors make themselves subordinate to courts that they normally have the power to oversee, at least in civil cases (Lü 2008; Sun 2009; LZM31102011). As one provincial-level judge and key coordinator in a provincial local environmental court’s structure explains: If I (as a prosecutor) were to do this (initiate environmental civil litigation) it would not enhance my power, but actually decrease it. If I am a plaintiff in this case and if I win ﬁnally, no problem. But what if I lose? Once that happens I can no longer exercise my supervisory right of prosecutorial protest (kangsu), whereas I can so in other cases, because I (as a prosecutor) supervise your (the court’s) work. (SFG10102011) Thus, criminal work remains the priority, and these civil cases are not deemed important enough, nor incentivized enough, to spur further specialization and the investment necessary to handle a larger volume and more complex and difﬁcult cases. In the present conditions it is, therefore, unlikely that China can follow Brazil. One might, however, wonder whether a legal change granting broad authority to initiate civil litigation to Chinese prosecutors would change this. One argument is that under the old pre-amended civil procedure law, prosecutors were challenged in proving they had a direct interest in the case, which made it harder to get these types of cases accepted in court. A change in the law might facilitate prosecutorial civil litigation and strengthen this form of prosecutorial environmental regulation (Liu 2011). On the other hand, the fact that no such standing existed made any attempt to initiate these cases and get them automatically deﬁned as a form of innovation worthy of a bonus. With the new law, perhaps more prosecutors will start such cases. However, based on all of the above, we very much doubt this. It seems that the only driver for such cases was the innovation bonus, which the amended law terminates (ZJW02112011). With the new law, all existing obstacles, such as lack of capacity, independence, and a preference for criminal work, remain.As such, we can only conclude that Chinese prosecutors are unlikely to become a strong regulatory force using civil litigation against polluters, similar to what has happened in Brazil. 5. Conclusion Brazil’s successful prosecutorial form of environmental regulation has its own historical, political, and legal condi- tions. Key in these conditions were a broad political reform and regime change that led to an institutional and legal reform creating broad prosecutorial independence and legal powers to protect the public interest (Mueller 2006; McAllister 2008). This paper has sought to understand whether successful prosecutorial environmental regulation could also occur in the Global South when similar conditions were not present. It has done so by studying the development of prosecutorial civil litigation against polluters in China. It ﬁnds that, while the Chinese cases studied here bear some resemblance to action in Brazil, their similarities are superﬁcial at best.
